topic: Area C: The World Around Us
subject: spanish

items.q: How do you say 'it is hot' when talking about the weather?
items.correct: 'Hace calor' - weather expressions use the verb hacer.
items.why: Spanish describes weather with hacer (hace sol, hace frío); copying the English be-structure produces unidiomatic and sometimes embarrassing sentences.

items.q: Can 'hay' and 'está' be used interchangeably for places?
items.correct: No - hay introduces the existence of something ('Hay un cine'), while está locates a specific known place ('El cine está cerca').
items.misconception: Hay and está both just mean 'is', so either works.
items.random_wrong: Está is only used for people, never places.
items.why: The existence/location distinction governs which verb is correct; mixing them is one of the most frequent errors in town descriptions.

items.q: What does 'actual' mean in Spanish, as in 'el problema actual'?
items.misconception: Actual or real, as in English.
items.why: 'Actual' is a false friend: Spanish for 'real/actual' is 'verdadero' or 'real', so texts about today's environmental issues are easily misread.

items.q: 'Gira a la derecha' tells you to do what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Turn right. “Turn left” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: It is hot. “It is cold” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How do you say 'there is a park in my town'?
items.correct: Hay un parque en mi ciudad
items.misconception: Está un parque en mi ciudad
items.random_wrong: Es un parque en mi ciudad
items.why: It's Hay un parque en mi ciudad. “Está un parque en mi ciudad” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Pollution. “Recycling” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
